turns.content: can you help me write an email to my lecturer asking for an extension... i literally cannot type this professionally right now im too frustrated

Of course! I can help you draft that. A few things that'll help me write it:

1. What's the assignment and when is it currently due?
2. How much extra time do you need?
3. What's the reason for the extension? (You don't have to share details with me, but knowing the general reason helps me frame it appropriately for the email.)

Also, what's the lecturer's name and do they prefer formal or more casual communication?

its my environmental policy essay due this friday and i need like 3-4 extra days. the reason is... ok so this is embarrassing but i have eczema and its absolutely destroyed my hands this week. like i can barely hold a pen and typing for long stretches is genuinely painful because the skin on my fingers keeps cracking open. it always gets worse when im stressed and guess what assignment season does to me lol

That's not embarrassing at all — eczema is a legitimate medical condition and cracked skin on your hands directly impacts your ability to write and type. That's a completely valid reason for an extension.

Here's a draft. The lecturer's name?

Actually, let me give you a template you can fill in:

---

Subject: Extension Request — Environmental Policy Essay

Dear [Professor/Dr. Last Name],

I'm writing to request a short extension on the environmental policy essay due this Friday [date]. I'm currently experiencing a significant flare-up of a chronic skin condition affecting my hands, which is making extended typing and writing very painful and slow.

I've been working on the essay and am confident in my ability to submit quality work. I would greatly appreciate an extension of 3-4 days, with a revised submission of [Tuesday/Wednesday — pick the date].

I'm happy to provide medical documentation if required. Thank you for your understanding.

Best regards,
[Your name]
[Student ID]

---

Does that tone work? Do you want to adjust anything?

thats actually perfect thank you. his name is dr Patterson. also i like that you said chronic skin condition instead of eczema because idk theres something about the word eczema that makes people think its just dry skin and not a big deal when actually my hands look like they belong to a horror movie right now

Yeah, "chronic skin condition" sounds more clinical and serious, which is what you want in a formal request. People unfortunately do underestimate eczema — they picture a little dry patch, not cracked bleeding hands.

So updated with Dr. Patterson:

---

Subject: Extension Request — Environmental Policy Essay

Dear Dr. Patterson,

I'm writing to request a short extension on the environmental policy essay due this Friday. I'm currently experiencing a significant flare-up of a chronic skin condition affecting my hands, which is making extended typing and writing very painful and slow.

I've been working on the essay and am confident in my ability to submit quality work. I would greatly appreciate an extension of 3-4 days, with a revised submission date of Tuesday the 24th.

I'm happy to provide medical documentation if required. Thank you for your understanding.

Best regards,
[Your name and student ID]

---

Have you been to a doctor or your uni's health service about it? Not just for the email — but because if it's that bad, they might be able to help with treatment too.

i have a gp but honestly the cycle is always the same. it flares up, i go get a stronger steroid cream, it calms down, then next time im stressed it comes back even worse. ive had it since i was a kid but it was just in my elbows and knees back then... now its my hands and sometimes my neck which is so much harder to deal with. i reckon my body just stores all my stress in my skin

The stress-flare cycle is really common with eczema and it's genuinely frustrating because the thing that triggers it (stress) is the hardest thing to control, especially during uni. And yeah, it migrating to hands and neck as you've gotten older is tough — those are areas that are constantly visible and in use.

A few things that might help beyond the steroid cream cycle:

- **Ask your GP about a management plan** specifically for flare prevention, not just treatment. Some people have success with regular moisturizing routines, antihistamines, or different triggers they haven't identified.
- **Your uni probably has disability/accessibility services.** If eczema regularly impacts your ability to complete work during high-stress periods, you might qualify for an ongoing accommodation plan. That way you wouldn't have to email lecturers every time — it'd be built into the system.
- **Cotton gloves at night** with a thick emollient underneath can help hands heal faster when they're really cracked.

Have you ever looked into the accessibility services thing? It could save you a lot of stress about extensions.

i actually didnt know i could register with accessibility services for eczema... i always thought that was for like more serious disabilities. but honestly it does wreck me every exam period without fail so maybe i should look into it. the cotton gloves thing i do already btw my mum buys them in bulk lol she worries heaps about my skin

Your mum buying cotton gloves in bulk is very sweet. And yes, definitely look into accessibility services — chronic conditions that flare up and impact your ability to study absolutely count. You don't need to be in a wheelchair to qualify. Eczema that regularly cracks your hands open during high-stress periods and stops you from typing is a genuine functional impairment.

Usually you just need a letter from your GP confirming the condition and how it affects you academically. Then the uni sets up a plan — things like automatic extension provisions, alternative exam arrangements, that kind of thing.

It would take so much pressure off knowing you have a safety net instead of scrambling for extensions every time it flares.

ok im genuinely going to do that in january when semester starts again. for now im just gonna send this email to dr patterson and then try to do some writing in short bursts with breaks to put cream on. the fun glamorous life of a uni student lol

The short bursts approach is smart — 20-25 minutes of writing, then a break to rest your hands and moisturize. You'll still make solid progress without pushing through the pain.

Send that email to Dr. Patterson now while you're thinking about it. And seriously, put a reminder in your phone for January to contact accessibility services. Future you during next exam season will be so grateful.

setting the reminder now... done. also i just realised how ironic it is that my thesis involves spending hours at a microscope categorising tiny plastic fragments which requires steady hands and fine motor control and my hands are literally falling apart. the universe has a sense of humour

Ha, that IS brutal irony. Microscopy with cracked fingers sounds genuinely miserable. If you're doing lab work over the break, those cotton gloves your mum gets you — some people cut the fingertips off and wear them while doing fine work. Keeps the moisture in on most of the hand while leaving your fingertips free for precision.

But yeah, one thing at a time. Email first, essay in short bursts, and get through this week. The lab work can wait until your hands have calmed down.

Hope your hands start feeling better soon. Take care of yourself!
will do. thanks for the email and the accessibility tip that was lowkey the most useful thing anyone has told me in ages. ok going to send this and then start writing... slowly lol
You've got this — slow and steady still gets the essay done. Good luck with it, and be kind to your hands! Hope Dr. Patterson comes through with that extension.
